subjective_review::goseg/docker/orchestr.summary: File changed since last review — re-review recommended

review::.::holistic::abstraction_fitness.summary: Runtime seam structs form broad function-table abstractions that add indirection but little domain behavior, increasing call depth without clear payoff.

evidence: `goseg/routines/runtime_seams.go` constructs `dockerRoutineRuntime` by assigning many fields directly to package functions (`getContainerState`, `updateContainerState`, `getShipStatus`, `startContainer`, etc.) without adding translation or policy.

`goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_seams.go` defines `dockerRuntime` with a large number of function fields and populates them as direct pass-throughs (`os.Open`, `os.ReadFile`, `config.UpdateContainerState`, `latestContainerImage`, etc.).

Execution paths in `goseg/docker/orchestration/wireguard.go` consume this runtime by passing the struct through orchestration calls (`loadWireguard(newWireguardRuntime())`, `buildWgConfWithRuntime(rt)`), so the seam depth is driven by infrastructure rather than behavior.
detail.suggestion: Replace broad function-table seams with small, cohesive interfaces at true abstraction boundaries (for example separate container-runtime, system, and config adapters), and keep direct package calls when no alternate implementation exists.

review::.::holistic::abstraction_fitness.note: not reported in latest holistic re-import

review::.::holistic::abstraction_fitness.summary: Collector dependencies are exposed as mutable global function vars, creating a brittle package-level abstraction leak instead of an explicit, testable dependency boundary.

evidence: `goseg/broadcast/collectors/collectors.go` declares a seam block of mutable package vars for most external interactions (`resolveBackupRoot`, `startramSettingsSnapshot`, `getShipStatus`, `getContainerStats`, `getContainerImageTag`, `startramRegionsFn`, etc.).
`ConstructPierInfo` and `collectUrbitViewInputs` consume these globals in core assembly paths rather than through an explicit interface passed at call time.
Broadcast entrypoints (`ConstructPierInfo` in `goseg/broadcast/broadcast.go`) call into these collectors directly, so callers depend on global mutable wiring state instead of explicit contracts.
detail.suggestion: Introduce a `CollectorRuntime` interface/struct constructed in one place and passed into collector entrypoints so each collector method has an explicit dependency set and avoids package-level mutation.

review::.::holistic::abstraction_fitness.summary: Config is used as a mutable process-wide singleton, so consumers inherit hidden coupling to initialization order and environment-backed globals.

evidence: `config/config.go` exposes mutable globals (`globalConfig`, `BasePath`, `Architecture`, `DebugMode`, `GSContainers`, `DockerDir`) that are read across modules.
`initializePaths` updates derived globals (`BasePath`, `confPath`, `keyPath`) at runtime, while helpers like `GetStoragePath` and `Conf` depend on that mutable surface.

Downstream modules rely on those globals indirectly through imported package functions (`config.BasePath` in wireguard path resolution and defaults/bootstrap flows), tying cross-cutting behavior to a single mutable source of truth.
Runtime package constructors and tests must reason about these implicit side effects rather than an explicit config object.
detail.suggestion: Create an explicit immutable runtime config context initialized once at startup and inject it where needed, leaving `config` as a narrow persistence adapter rather than the global source of process behavior.

review::.::holistic::abstraction_fitness.note: not reported in latest holistic re-import

review::.::holistic::abstraction_fitness.summary: Broadcast serialization mutates the domain object for protocol metadata instead of using a dedicated transport abstraction.

evidence: `GetStateJson` in `goseg/broadcast/broadcast.go` mutates `bState.Type` and `bState.AuthLevel` before marshalling.
`BroadcastToClients` fetches domain state (`GetState`) and sends it to `GetStateJson` directly, coupling domain state shape to wire envelope requirements.
The `//temp` comments in the serializer indicate policy that should be owned by a dedicated response-mapping layer, not core state type semantics.
detail.suggestion: Add an explicit outbound DTO/envelope type for websocket transport and keep `AuthBroadcast` as a pure domain model, so serialization and protocol tags are applied in one dedicated mapper.

review::.::holistic::design_coherence::S.summary: Support ticket ingestion, bug payload collection, archive creation, upload, and transition orchestration are tightly coupled in `SupportHandler`.

evidence: `SupportHandler` calls `json.Unmarshal`, creates directories, invokes `dumpBugReport`, optionally captures CPU profile, zips outputs, deletes temp dirs, submits HTTP upload, and emits transition state events in one function.
The same file also exposes helpers (`zipDir`, `captureCPUProfile`, `sendBugReport`, `lastTwoLogs`) that are only loosely related to request-level control flow and hard to test independently.
`Config/support` style behavior is coupled to `logger` internals (`logger.LogPath`) and filesystem assumptions, instead of being injected or coordinated through a dedicated coordinator.
detail.suggestion: Extract a small support-bugreport service with explicit stages (`prepare -> collect -> package -> transmit`) and separate adapters for filesystem, docker-log collection, and transport. Keep `SupportHandler` as orchestration with typed inputs/outputs, not a monolith that owns every stage.

review::.::holistic::design_coherence::S.note: not reported in latest holistic re-import

review::.::holistic::design_coherence::S.summary: Multiple modules rely on stringly-typed workflow/control signals and error text matching instead of typed outcomes.

evidence: `runImportUploadPipeline` classifies failure behavior via `strings.HasPrefix(err.Error(), "Failed to configure imported pier")`, creating behavior coupling to exact message text.
`processAction` branches on payload `Type` using raw strings (`"login"`, `"logout"`, `"password"`), while other modules publish string statuses/events instead of typed constants/enums.
Container and state transitions in `shipworkflow`/`routines` are also driven by raw status/action/event strings spread across different files.
detail.suggestion: Introduce shared typed enums/events for operation states and action outcomes (e.g., `ImportPhase`, `LeakAction`, `ShipLifecycleEvent`, `TransitionResult`) and replace string parsing/switching with explicit status/result types.

review::.::holistic::design_coherence::S.note: not reported in latest holistic re-import

review::.::holistic::design_coherence::B.summary: Bug-report artifact path construction is inconsistent and can bypass configured base path resolution.

evidence: Fallback path logic in `makeBugReportPath` returns `filepath.Join(config.BasePath, "/bug-reports/")`, where `"/bug-reports/"` is treated as absolute and drops `config.BasePath`.
Elsewhere in the same codebase, base-path assembly is performed differently (relative join/concatenation), so support artifacts and logs can diverge by filesystem root.
This makes artifact location policy less predictable and increases deployment-specific behavior risk.
detail.suggestion: Normalize path-building through one utility (or config-backed policy) that always treats suffix segments as relative, and centralize artifact-root selection so support, logs, and exports follow one rule.

review::.::holistic::error_consistency::.summary: `KillContainerUsingPort` can report success when container stop fails, masking port-reclaim failures during startup.

evidence: `KillContainerUsingPort` iterates matching containers and on `cli.ContainerStop` error logs only (`zap.L().Error`) and does not return that error.
In the same function, it returns `nil` immediately after attempting stop on first match even if stop failed.
`lifecycle.Initialize` calls `network.KillContainerUsingPort(80)` and only logs the error, so upstream startup is not blocked by failed port cleanup.
detail.suggestion: Change `KillContainerUsingPort` to only return nil when all required teardown steps succeed; return the stop error when a stop attempt fails and let callers decide severity. In `lifecycle.Initialize`, fail hard or route to explicit degraded-mode recovery when port cleanup fails.

review::.::holistic::error_consistency::.summary: Top-level startup boundary treats subsystem initialization errors inconsistently, enabling partially initialized runtime without clear failure contract.

evidence: `main.go` exits on `config.Initialize()` error but only logs errors for many later initializers and continues startup.
`broadcast.Initialize()` can return bootstrap errors (`unable to initialize broadcast state`) while `main.go` does not propagate these as startup failures.
`main.go` starts the server and long-running routines even after non-fatal-logged failures in multiple dependencies, increasing risk of nil/partial state behavior at runtime.
detail.suggestion: Introduce a consistent init contract (fatal vs recoverable) and enforce it at the main boundary. Return/aggregate initialization errors from each subsystem, and decide deterministic policy: either stop startup on mandatory init failures or record state and suppress route/routine registration until dependency checks pass.

review::.::holistic::abstraction_fitness.summary: Runtime seam interfaces in production are largely thin forwarding wrappers, adding indirection without policy or behavioral branching.

evidence: `broadcast/broadcast.go` defines an interface and default implementation for client-runtime methods, then funnels calls through a tiny forwarding layer.
`routines/runtime_seams.go` and `docker/orchestration/runtime_seams.go` each declare explicit seam interfaces and default implementations instead of using direct call sites.
`routines/docker.go` relies on these seam calls, so normal execution pays extra abstraction depth for behavior that is not currently polymorphic.
Packet abstraction analysis reports repeated indirection hotspots in these files with max chain depth 4, matching the observed seam-stack shape.
detail.suggestion: Replace pass-through wrappers with direct package-level collaborators or function variables only in test-critical places, and keep interfaces only where multiple real implementations exist; fold duplicated seam definitions into a small shared seam strategy to reduce wrapper depth and coupling.

review::.::holistic::design_coherence::o.summary: Support and routine handlers mix request orchestration, container lifecycle transitions, and cleanup side effects in one layer, reducing boundary clarity and making behavior changes brittle.

evidence: `SupportHandler` orchestrates transport/session parsing, filesystem preparation, archiving/upload, status transitions, and deferred cleanup in the same flow.
`DockerSubscriptionHandlerWithRuntime` and related docker runtime handlers also perform state transition logic alongside container command execution paths.
This creates repeated orchestration logic patterns without a dedicated command/orchestrator abstraction.
detail.suggestion: Introduce a dedicated orchestration service (e.g., `SupportOperationCoordinator`) that encapsulates the end-to-end flow, delegates I/O to adapters, and returns explicit operation outcomes; keep handlers as thin dispatchers and move transition publishing/cleanup into explicit post-steps.

review::.::holistic::design_coherence::i.summary: The importer module couples transport/session validation, state persistence, runtime bootstrap sequencing, and asynchronous follow-up in one implementation, making sequencing rules hard to evolve safely.

Tests cover individual steps but the design has no explicit orchestrator object to encode phase transitions and invariant checks centrally.
Post-import behavior includes async execution via `startImportedPierPostImportPipeline`, which further splits lifecycle concerns across unrelated callsites.
detail.suggestion: Split importer responsibilities into explicit phases (`UploadSessionStore`, `ImportPlanner`, `ImportExecutor`, `PostImportOrchestrator`) and route all phase transitions through one coordinator with typed state machine transitions and a single failure/retry policy.

review::.::holistic::design_coherence::t.summary: Residual TODO markers in auth/importer/session workflows indicate deferred lifecycle/invariant work, which suggests incomplete design closure across related modules.

`goseg/shipworkflow/urbit_operations.go` has TODO around scry data sourcing rather than injecting a dedicated abstraction.
These deferred points sit in the same feature path that already mixes transport concerns with lifecycle mechanics.

review::.::holistic::design_coherence::t.note: not reported in latest holistic re-import

review::.::holistic::error_consistency::.summary: Boundary functions mix raw error returns with wrapped/contextual errors, creating inconsistent failure contracts.

evidence: In `goseg/config/wireguard.go`, `GetWgConf` and `CreateDefaultWGConf` return raw underlying errors (`return wgConf, err`, `return err`) for I/O/encode failures without operation context.
In `goseg/click/backup/service.go`, `backupAgent` returns `err` directly from `executeClickCommandForBackup`, while nearby failures are wrapped with command/component context before returning.

In `goseg/docker/lifecycle/lifecycle.go`, several container runtime helpers (`createAndStartContainer`, parts of `GetShipStatus`, and other wrappers) return raw `err` despite the surrounding flow being heavily contextualized.
detail.suggestion: Establish a shared boundary error pattern (for example, wrap all external I/O/command failures with `%w` and a consistent operation prefix) and apply it across these modules. This improves logs, `errors.Is`/`errors.As` usability, and failure triage consistency.

review::.::holistic::error_consistency::.summary: Critical startup paths use hard process termination instead of a consistent error propagation contract.

evidence: In `goseg/main.go`, `init()` executes `panic(err)` when embedded content setup fails (`fs.Sub` error), skipping normal error reporting and shutdown paths.
In `goseg/main.go`, `killSwitch()` calls `os.Exit(0)` on debug mode, terminating the process directly from a loop routine instead of returning an error through caller control.
The same startup layer otherwise uses normal `return`/logging-based error handling for other subsystem failures, so fatal handling strategy is inconsistent across adjacent startup code paths.
detail.suggestion: Refactor bootstrap to keep one failure contract: return structured errors from initialization entry points, route them through a single central error handler/logger, and avoid direct `panic`/`os.Exit` in favor of cancellation/cleanup-aware shutdown.

review::.::holistic::abstraction_fitness.summary: `main.go` mixes process orchestration policy, subsystem lifecycle sequencing, configuration control flow, and long-running service startup in one function, making composition hard to reason about and weakly abstracted.

evidence: `main.go` `main()` directly performs initialization (`config.Initialize`, `broadcast.Initialize`, `docker.Initialize`) and immediately starts many background workers (`CheckVersionLoop`, `AptUpdateLoop`, `DockerListenerWithContext`, multiple rectify handlers, leak/startup loops).

CLI argument handling and runtime behavior (`--http-port`, `dev`) are coupled with boot orchestration and scheduling decisions (`killSwitch`, version update path, WG/MinIO/Llama load paths) in the same unit.
No clear orchestrator abstraction exists to declare startup dependencies and shutdown ordering; orchestration is implicit in procedural code.
detail.suggestion: Introduce an explicit `Bootstrap`/`RuntimeCoordinator` component with a lifecycle interface that owns startup graph ordering, dependency readiness barriers, and policy flags; keep `main()` as a thin argument parser that composes components and invokes the coordinator.

review::.::holistic::abstraction_fitness.note: not reported in latest holistic re-import

review::.::holistic::abstraction_fitness.summary: Auth state management is exposed as mutable package-global state (`ClientManager`), forcing other modules to depend on global variables instead of stable interfaces.

evidence: `auth.go` declares exported mutable singleton `var ClientManager = NewClientManager()`.
`broadcast/broadcast.go` writes through `auth.ClientManager.BroadcastAuth` and `.BroadcastUnauth` directly rather than via an injected abstraction.
`importer/importer.go` uses `auth.TokenIdAuthed(auth.ClientManager, token.ID)` instead of an auth service interface.
detail.suggestion: Encapsulate client manager behind an internal interface (e.g., `AuthBus`) and inject it into consumers; keep `ClientManager` unexported or readonly, expose constructor/setter hooks for tests, and use getter methods everywhere to avoid global variable coupling.

review::.::holistic::abstraction_fitness.summary: Runtime indirection is layered repeatedly through function field bundles and mutable defaults, creating high indirection cost with limited abstraction payoff.

evidence: `routines/runtime_seams.go` defines multiple small function-injected structs for nearly every interaction (`dockerRoutineContainerOps`, `dockerRoutineBroadcastOps`, `dockerRoutineShipOps`, `runtimeBase` wrappers).

`docker/orchestration/runtime_seams.go` adds analogous runtime structs and no-op defaults (`createDefaultWGConfFn`, `getWgConfFn`) in `newDockerRuntime`, overriding behavior only in narrow constructors like `newWireguardRuntime`.
Lifecycle seams in `docker/lifecycle/lifecycle.go` expose global mutable function hooks (`SetImagePuller`, `SetContainerConfigResolver`) plus package-level state used to alter behavior externally.
detail.suggestion: Replace repeated single-purpose closure bundles with explicit interfaces for narrow ports (e.g., container lifecycle, config snapshot, broadcast transport) and concrete implementations, reducing layers of pass-through indirection and making seam ownership explicit.

review::.::holistic::abstraction_fitness.note: not reported in latest holistic re-import

review::.::holistic::abstraction_fitness.summary: Static, versioned domain data is embedded as large inline structs/JSON strings, making release and policy updates coupled to recompilation and weakening abstraction boundaries between defaults and behavior.

evidence: `defaults/defaults.go` centralizes many operational defaults in package globals (`UrbitConfig`, `SysConfig`, `WgConfig`) directly tied to runtime composition.
`defaults/version.go` stores the full default version matrix in a giant `DefaultVersionText` JSON literal and unmarshals at init instead of loading through a data artifact boundary.
`config/config.go` merges these defaults with loaded runtime config during `initializeConfig`, so changing defaults requires source edits and rebuild for data updates.
detail.suggestion: Externalize version/config defaults into typed, versioned data files or a small manifest service loader and validate schema at startup, keeping code modules focused on orchestration/policy and minimizing recompilation dependence for environment/version policy updates.

review::.::holistic::abstraction_fitness.note: not reported in latest holistic re-import

review::.::holistic::design_coherence::s.summary: `SupportHandler` couples transport parsing, report assembly, artifact collection, archiving, and submission in one monolithic flow, making behavior changes brittle and hard to reason about.

evidence: `SupportHandler` unmarshals support payloads, creates report directories, orchestrates log collection via `dumpBugReport`, optionally profiles CPU, zips the result, deletes temp artifacts, publishes transition states, and posts the bug report in a single function.
The same module also implements low-level helpers (`dumpBugReport`, `dumpDockerLogs`, `zipDir`, `sendBugReport`) so a single edit touches multiple concerns with strong temporal coupling.
detail.suggestion: Extract a `BugReportCoordinator` that owns request validation and workflow orchestration, and move I/O concerns into smaller collaborators (artifact collector, zip assembler, transport sender, transition emitter) with explicit inputs/outputs. Keep `SupportHandler` as a thin adapter.

review::.::holistic::design_coherence::s.note: not reported in latest holistic re-import

review::.::holistic::design_coherence::u.summary: The upload endpoint handler intermixes HTTP transport concerns with business workflow, making request handling and import orchestration tightly coupled.

evidence: `HTTPUploadHandler` configures response headers, handles OPTIONS/CORS, request context timeout, validates session headers/path vars, reads multipart chunks, runs pipeline execution, emits status transitions, and writes completion/error HTTP responses.

Pipeline helpers invoked from this function (`validateUploadRequest`, `runImportUploadPipeline`, `persistUploadedChunk`, `combineChunksWithTimeout`, `finalizeUploadOnCompletion`) are still called inline from the same handler, so transport and orchestration are inseparable in practice.
detail.suggestion: Separate request adaptation from orchestration by introducing a `ValidatedUploadRequest` command object and an `ImportUploadService` interface; keep handler focused on mapping HTTP I/O to service calls and status serialization.

review::.::holistic::design_coherence::u.note: not reported in latest holistic re-import

review::.::holistic::design_coherence::g.summary: Many packages use ad-hoc package-level function vars for dependency seams, creating a fragmented and mutable abstraction style with higher cognitive overhead than needed.

evidence: `system/disk.go` and `system/wifi.go` use mutable function vars (`runDiskCommandFn`, `execCommandForWiFi`, etc.) and exec/call aliases at package level for test seams.

`docker/orchestration/runtime_seams.go` and `docker/lifecycle/lifecycle.go` define broad runtime operation structs/vars that proxy core calls and are overridden in tests, while `config/mc.go` and `shipworkflow/startram.go` add more single-function indirection points.
The same seam pattern is repeated differently across modules instead of a single coherent dependency strategy, which spreads override points and blurs ownership of orchestration boundaries.
detail.suggestion: Replace the ad-hoc global function-var seam pattern with explicit runtime interfaces passed through constructors (or a shared seam/context object) for modules that need replaceable collaborators, and keep per-module seams minimal and uniform.

review::.::holistic::design_coherence::g.note: not reported in latest holistic re-import

review::.::holistic::design_coherence::b.summary: Broadcast snapshot construction leaks mutable system state maps directly into snapshot output, weakening isolation between data producer and emitter.

evidence: `systemInfoCollector.collect` assigns `sysInfo.Info.SMART = system.SmartResults` directly from a package-level mutable map, rather than cloning at collection time.
`goseg/system/disk.go` maintains `SmartResults` as mutable shared package state, so downstream collectors can observe mutable internal maps without an explicit contract boundary.
detail.suggestion: Introduce immutable snapshot accessors on `system` (for drives/SMART/status data), and clone maps in the collector so emitted broadcast data is detached from mutable package internals.

review::.::holistic::abstraction_fitness.summary: Config is exposed through many narrow snapshot structs that duplicate a copy-on-read pattern, creating low-leverage abstraction debt across package boundaries.

evidence: Each feature area defines independent snapshot views over `Conf()` (`goseg/config/config.go:279-377`).

Collector and routine layers create helper shims that exist solely to wrap those snapshots without domain logic (`goseg/broadcast/collectors/collectors.go:32-38`, `goseg/routines/runtime_seams.go:44-48`, `89-93`).
Higher-level orchestration code directly depends on many snapshot functions (`goseg/importer/importer.go:69-78`, `goseg/main.go:92-104`) instead of a bounded config contract.
detail.suggestion: Replace the ad-hoc snapshot family with bounded read interfaces (for example `ShipConfigView`, `RuntimeConfigView`, `AuthConfigView`) and inject explicit dependencies at subsystem boundaries, so each caller can request only the small set of fields it truly needs with clear ownership and stronger API semantics.

review::.::holistic::abstraction_fitness.note: not reported in latest holistic re-import

review::.::holistic::abstraction_fitness.summary: Runtime-seam layers and helper wrappers are stacked broadly for testability but add little domain behavior and increase trace complexity.

evidence: Broadcast exposes a custom runtime struct with many function fields plus defaulting logic, yet execution remains a thin pass-through sequence (`goseg/broadcast/loop.go:13-40`, `49-83`).
Docker routines mirror the same style with multiple nested seam structs and direct forwarding (`goseg/routines/runtime_seams.go:24-65`, `84-103`).

detail.suggestion: Constrain seams to true boundaries only (one seam per subsystem boundary), and replace pass-through function buckets with direct interfaces that carry one clear behavioral contract; avoid per-event/per-call wrapper reconstruction for hot paths.

review::.::holistic::abstraction_fitness.note: not reported in latest holistic re-import

review::.::holistic::abstraction_fitness.summary: Default no-op seam implementations in orchestration/runtime construction obscure whether required dependencies are actually configured.

review::.::holistic::abstraction_fitness.note: not reported in latest holistic re-import

review::.::holistic::abstraction_fitness.summary: Runtime seam structs add multiple indirection layers but currently expose no real behavioral abstraction, reducing readability and creating maintenance overhead.

evidence: `dockerRuntime` and `dockerRoutineRuntime` are large function-field aggregates (`BasePath`, `Architecture`, `fileOps`, `containerOps`, `imageOps`, `configOps`, etc.) and most production code still flows through these wrappers.

Production constructors (`newWireguardRuntime`, `newUrbitRuntime`, `newMinIORuntime`) build mostly identical seam objects and immediately pass them into call chains that eventually call nearly same underlying package functions.
Call paths in loaders (`loadWireguard`, `loadUrbits`) and Docker routines are driven by these runtime wrappers rather than direct service-level contracts.
detail.suggestion: Refactor to narrow seam interfaces to only behavior that is truly variable (e.g., shell/docker/test doubles), and keep fixed dependencies directly at call sites. review::.::holistic::abstraction_fitness.note: not reported in latest holistic re-import

review::.::holistic::abstraction_fitness.summary: Global configuration values are used as an implicit, mutable bag across modules, weakening abstraction boundaries and making call contexts hard to reason about.

Higher-level flows in `shipworkflow` and routines directly read `config` snapshots and then mutate config through `UpdateConfTyped` without an explicit boundary object.
detail.suggestion: Introduce an explicit immutable runtime context object (or interface) passed into orchestrators/workflows, containing only fields actually needed per operation (paths, architecture, mode), and keep mutation operations behind small repository-style interfaces to reduce ambient global dependency.

review::.::holistic::abstraction_fitness.summary: Multiple files layer pass-through proxy functions over underlying dependencies without policy, creating low-leverage indirection chains.

`version` and orchestration-related seam files similarly wrap existing calls instead of exposing fewer, policy-bearing interfaces, increasing chain depth with limited abstraction leverage.
detail.suggestion: Collapse trivial pass-through wrappers into direct calls unless they encode test-time behavior or translation. For truly replaceable boundaries (e.g., command execution), replace proxy variables with a concise interface and inject a single mock/fake implementation.

review::.::holistic::cross_module_archit.summary: Protocol version constant is a dead artifact and does not enforce cross-module compatibility at the action boundary.

evidence: `ProtocolVersion` is declared in `goseg/protocol/actions/actions.go` with no references outside that file.

Upload action intake in `goseg/uploadsvc/adapters/upload_payload.go` validates only action token strings, and `goseg/uploadsvc/service.go` routes via `actions.ParseUploadAction` without any explicit protocol version check.
There is no version gate before dispatch and no compatibility fail-fast path at this boundary.
detail.suggestion: Either remove `ProtocolVersion` or enforce it in the websocket action intake path (e.g., include version in incoming payload/header, validate in `ParseAction`/adapter layer, and reject mismatched clients before dispatch) to keep protocol contract checks centralized.
detail.investigation:
# Issue a9608386 investigation
- Reviewed actions/uploadsvc boundary and confirmed ProtocolVersion constant had no references outside actions module.
- Existing payload schema only validates action tokens and dispatches via ParseUploadAction; no protocol version in ingress path.
- Removed dead ProtocolVersion constant from  to eliminate stale contract artifact without introducing an incomplete pseudo-check.

review::.::holistic::cross_module_archit.summary: An extra click-level response shim exists separately from the active runtime parser path, creating duplicated boundaries and a stale module entry point.

evidence: `goseg/click/filter.go` defines `filterResponse` as a one-line pass-through to `response.ParsePokeResponse` and retains commented legacy logic.
Production call paths in `goseg/click/restore/restore.go` depend on `runtime.FilterResponse`, not `filterResponse` in `goseg/click/filter.go`.
A live parser boundary already exists in `goseg/click/internal/runtime/runtime.go`, making `filterResponse` an additional unconsumed indirection layer.
detail.suggestion: Delete or retire `goseg/click/filter.go` and standardize on a single parser boundary (`runtime.FilterResponse` or another explicit shared abstraction), then remove the duplicate shim and commented legacy block.

review::.::holistic::abstraction_fitness.summary: Thin runtime wrappers add indirection without enforcing policy across broadcast/version/orchestration entrypoints.

evidence: `ApplyBroadcastUpdate` is a compatibility shim that only wraps `ApplyBroadcastTransition` with a `functionTransition` and no additional policy.
`callUpdater` and `updateDocker` in `routines/version.go` only forward to `*WithRuntime(newVersionRuntime(), ...)` variants.
`LoadWireguard` and `WriteWgConf` are one-line delegators to `wireguardRuntime` functions.
This same shape appears in multiple modules and contributes to a consistent `max_chain_depth=4` indirection profile reported in seam-heavy files.
detail.suggestion: Keep only one exported/public entrypoint per concrete behavior and drop compatibility aliases; keep runtime-override seams private-to-tests. For production paths, call the concrete implementation directly through a single seam function instead of repeated `A -> AWithRuntime` wrappers.

review::.::holistic::abstraction_fitness.summary: Runtime interfaces are over-declared with only default implementations, limiting interface leverage.

evidence: `runtime_seams.go` defines many interfaces (contextOps, containerOps, commandOps, etc.) each with only one default implementation used by construction.
`routines/runtime_seams.go` similarly defines multiple small interfaces (`stateOps`, `containerOps`, `broadcastOps`, etc.) where production always uses `*Default` structs.
`shipworkflow/startram.go` uses a function-bag `startramRuntime` with mostly direct package function bindings and no observable strategy selection in production.
The patterns are duplicated across modules, inflating maintenance surface while adding little polymorphic value.
detail.suggestion: For modules with no alternate production implementations, replace broad interface hierarchies with compact function-set structs or plain closure fields; reserve true interfaces only where multiple providers are expected and documented.

review::.::holistic::abstraction_fitness.summary: Global auth singleton is exposed as a mutable object, leaking policy boundaries through `GetClientManager()`.

evidence: `GetClientManager()` returns the package-global `*structs.ClientManager` directly with no interface abstraction.
`broadcast` and other callers invoke behavior by directly reaching through this pointer (`auth.GetClientManager().BroadcastAuth`, `auth.GetClientManager().HasAuthSession()`).
Shared mutable singleton escape makes callers depend on full concrete type instead of a small behavioral contract, reducing abstraction boundary clarity.
detail.suggestion: Introduce a narrow `AuthManager` interface (e.g., `HasAuthSession`, `BroadcastAuth`, `AddAuthClient`, `BroadcastUnauth`) and inject it into runtimes/handlers, keeping the concrete singleton private to `auth` package internals.

review::.::holistic::abstraction_fitness.note: not reported in latest holistic re-import

review::.::holistic::cross_module_archit.summary: Config module uses mutable package-level function indirection seams that blur boundary contracts and invite hidden behavioral drift.

evidence: Runtime functions in `goseg/config/wireguard.go` depend on package-local vars (`confForWG`, `getVersionChannelForWG`, `wgKeyGenForCycle`, `updateConfTypedForCycle`) instead of direct dependencies.

`goseg/config/wireguard_test.go` resets and reassigns those vars to stub behavior in multiple tests, showing cross-file behavior is coupled to mutable package globals rather than explicit constructor/runtime seams.
detail.suggestion: Replace package-global function indirection with explicit dependency injection via constructor args or interfaces at the boundary, and keep package internals immutable in production; keep tests using dedicated test seams/fakes passed in through test-only constructors.
detail.investigation:
# Issue 16d95234 investigation
- Production code in `goseg/config/wireguard.go` used mutable package-level function vars (`confForWG`, `getVersionChannelForWG`, `wgKeyGenForCycle`, `updateConfTypedForCycle`) as indirection seams.
- Replaced those globals with a `wireguardRuntime` struct with immutable defaults in `newWireguardRuntime`.
- Refactored `UpdateWGConf`/`CycleWgKey` to delegate to internal helpers that receive explicit runtime dependencies.
- Updated tests to inject behavior by constructing and mutating a runtime value instead of mutating package globals.

review::.::holistic::cross_module_archit.summary: Unknown action namespaces are not explicitly classified, weakening contract enforcement at parser boundaries.

evidence: `ParseAction` in `goseg/protocol/actions/actions.go` obtains allowed actions via map indexing and loops over the result without handling missing namespace keys before iteration.

The error path returns an unsupported action error with the provided action but no explicit namespace-rejection path, so invalid namespace inputs can collapse into an ambiguous unsupported-action signal.
detail.suggestion: Add an explicit namespace validation branch in `ParseAction` and return a distinct namespace-level contract error (with namespace included) before action lookup, then update tests to assert this boundary behavior.

review::.::holistic::cross_module_archit.note: not reported in latest holistic re-import

review::.::holistic::cross_module_archit.summary: Protocol action version is declared as a contract constant but is not enforced at module boundaries, creating docs/runtime drift.

evidence: The protocol version constant is defined in `goseg/protocol/actions/actions.go` but only declared there; listed runtime/adaptation files do not consume it for validation.

`README.md` describes the websocket contract version, yet enforcement point in the request-adaptation/dispatch path (`upload_payload_test.go` scope) is token parsing and dispatch only, not version compatibility gating.
detail.suggestion: Either remove the unused version field or enforce it explicitly at websocket ingress/contract-check boundaries and in compatibility-gate tests so version drift cannot silently pass through while tests verify mismatch behavior.

review::.::holistic::cross_module_archit.note: not reported in latest holistic re-import

review::.::holistic::design_coherence::r.summary: Duplicate runtime seam abstraction layers split responsibility for the same Docker-bound contracts and increase design drift risk.

evidence: `dockerRuntime` in `goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_seams.go` and `dockerRoutineRuntime` in `goseg/routines/runtime_seams.go` each aggregate overlapping concerns like container operations, urbit config access, timers, and state transitions.

Both files define local default implementations (`dockerRuntime*Default`, `dockerRoutine*Default`) and separate constructor paths (`new...RuntimeWithContext`) that mirror each other instead of sharing a common seam baseline.
Lower layers still pull from these duplicated seam surfaces (`docker` lifecycle code and routine handlers), so any interface change must be propagated manually across two abstractions.
detail.suggestion: Consolidate seam contracts into a single shared package (or common base interface set) and compose domain-specific extensions per subsystem so each runtime boundary has one canonical interface graph and one default constructor path.

review::.::holistic::design_coherence::r.note: not reported in latest holistic re-import

review::.::holistic::design_coherence::i.summary: Import orchestration is implemented as a monolithic coordinator with mixed lifecycle, I/O, status publication, and cleanup logic.

evidence: `HTTPUploadHandler` and `runImportUploadPipeline` include auth/session validation, chunk I/O persistence, phase progression, and response/error publication in one flow.
`runImportedPierWorkflow` composes lifecycle steps while `configureUploadedPier` and `finalizeImportedPierReadiness` immediately trigger async readiness/read registration paths.
Similar orchestration style appears in ship operations (`runPackLifecycle`/`packMeld` patterns) with local transition/error closures and direct container/control commands in the same function scope.
detail.suggestion: Introduce a shared workflow engine for import/ship operations with explicit phases (`prepare`, `execute`, `post_process`, `rollback`) and adapter interfaces for side effects; keep command handlers as thin wrappers around pipeline composition.

review::.::holistic::design_coherence::i.note: not reported in latest holistic re-import

review::.::holistic::design_coherence::s.summary: Bug report support handler combines request handling, artifact generation, transition emission, and transport into one broad routine.

evidence: `SupportHandlerWithRuntime` performs payload parsing, runtime dir/file creation, optional CPU profiling, zip assembly, network report submission, and state transition signaling in one method.
Inline `handleError` closure mixes error mapping with transition publication side effects, while cleanup and completion publishing are handled via `defer` in the same function.
Transitions are emitted in a handler-centric style similar to workflow functions, indicating the same coupling pattern (control + transport + presentation state) already spreads across modules.
detail.suggestion: Refactor into separate services: request validation, bug-report artifact builder, sender (HTTP transport), and transition coordinator; keep `SupportHandlerWithRuntime` as orchestration of those components only.

review::.::holistic::design_coherence::s.note: not reported in latest holistic re-import

review::.::holistic::design_coherence::d.summary: Docker event switch handler combines policy decisions, state mutation, restart orchestration, and broadcast publication without separation of concerns.

evidence: `dockerSubscriptionHandlerWithRuntime` performs transition/state mutations and invokes `BroadcastToClients` and restart actions directly in each case arm.
`transition.DockerActionDie` and `transition.DockerActionStop` branches include branching for disable-restart and asynchronous restarts inline with state mutation logic.
Broadcast emission appears as repeated side-effect boilerplate across cases rather than delegated to a stable post-processing layer.
detail.suggestion: Replace large switch body with per-event handlers or a strategy map that cleanly separates state mutation, restart policy, and notification side effects; move common notification fanout to a shared post-handler hook.
detail.investigation:
# Issue 2cabcb85 investigation
- The Docker subscription handler mixed transition state updates, restart policy decisions, and client broadcast side effects in a large switch statement.
- I introduced a `dockerSubscriptionPlan` abstraction in `goseg/routines/docker.go` to separate core container-state transition logic from event-specific post-processing.
- I centralized broadcast emission into `publishDockerTransition`, with each handler producing one shared post-processing path.
- I split stop/start/die handling into dedicated functions (`dockerStopTransition`, `dockerStartTransition`, `dockerDieTransition`) and explicit after-transition hooks for wireguard updates and ship restart/cleanup behavior.
- I changed `updateContainerTransition` to return the updated container state so post-transition hooks can operate on fresh state.

review::.::holistic::abstraction_fitness.summary: Domain runtime wrappers in orchestration add type-level indirection without new behavior, creating weak abstractions and unnecessary coupling.

evidence: Domain seam constructors in `runtime_seams.go` return structs that are thin aliases/wrappers around `dockerRuntime` and do not define domain-specific methods.
In domain orchestration files, implementations primarily call the embedded base runtime methods instead of a domain-specific interface/strategy, so the wrapper types do not encode meaningful policy.
The same construction pattern appears to be repeated across several domain runtimes, indicating an abstraction hotspot rather than isolated naming convenience.
detail.suggestion: Replace the fake per-domain seams with a single shared runtime abstraction plus explicit capability interfaces (for operations that truly differ by domain), or consolidate into one runtime interface with small strategy objects. Keep domain wrappers only when they encapsulate behavior, not as pure pass-through aliases.

review::.::holistic::abstraction_fitness.summary: Constructor context is partially injected, but implementations still read global/module-level dependency points, weakening interface boundaries.

evidence: Runtime seam constructors accept snapshot-like inputs yet the runtime path continues to rely on broad package-level config/state access, so callers cannot fully control behavior through the constructor boundary.
This pattern appears in both routines and orchestration seam layers, so the same abstraction boundary is duplicated with the same leakage.
The seam is therefore only nominal: it suggests testability and separation but does not fully honor it.
detail.suggestion: Thread all required dependencies through the runtime struct (or constructor interface) and remove hidden package-global reads from downstream methods; define narrower interfaces (e.g., ConfigPort, FsPort, NetworkPort) and inject implementations.

review::.::holistic::abstraction_fitness.summary: Importer cleanup bypasses configurable filesystem abstraction by hardcoding `/tmp` instead of using initialized temp/storage context.

evidence: In the importer flow, `configureUploadedPier()` unconditionally defers `RemoveMultipartFiles("/tmp")`, while runtime initialization in the same module manages configurable upload/temp locations.
This makes path policy non-uniform across modules and breaks the intention of context-driven configuration used elsewhere in the import path.
The cleanup behavior therefore becomes an implicit, environment-specific contract instead of explicit dependency injection.
detail.suggestion: Inject the temp/upload base path from initialized context and route cleanup through a filesystem seam or helper interface so tests and alternate runtimes can control where temporary files are created and removed.

review::.::holistic::cross_module_archit.summary: Action-contract registry is outside documented runtime contract gate coverage despite being a shared boundary.

evidence: `README.md` documents runtime contract gate inputs and excludes `goseg/protocol/actions/actions.go`.
`goseg/protocol/actions/actions.go` defines canonical action tokens and validation for upload/c2c namespaces used by other modules, so governance coverage has a boundary blind spot.
detail.suggestion: Add `goseg/protocol/actions/actions.go` (and its test target) to the Runtime Contract Gate inputs and update the contract-gate script/checklist to treat action set changes as contract changes requiring paired tests.

review::.::holistic::cross_module_archit.summary: Shared action parser/registry has no direct boundary tests, weakening cross-module contract enforcement.

evidence: The listed module provides `ParseAction`, namespace lists, and `UnsupportedActionError` mapping logic used across transports, yet there is no direct `_test` in that package in the reviewed surface.

`goseg/uploadsvc/adapters/upload_payload_test.go` validates adapter mapping for known cases but does not independently assert parser invariants (namespace validation/error semantics order/casing/unsupported behavior) of the shared actions contract.
detail.suggestion: Create protocol-level tests for `ParseAction`, `SupportedActions`, and namespace-specific invalid/duplicate/unknown behavior, then lock these to the adapter/service expected contract through a shared parity test pattern.

review::.::holistic::design_coherence::d.summary: Duplicate support-reporting implementations in two packages increase drift risk and weaken module cohesion.

evidence: Both files define `supportRuntime` and `SupportHandlerWithRuntime` with the same orchestration shape and same bug-report workflow (message parse → state transitions → dump/zip/send).
Both files contain the same constants and transitions for `bugReport`/`bugReportError` and invoke the same publish/send pipeline, creating parallel ownership.
detail.suggestion: Extract the support orchestration into a single package-owned module (for example `systemsupport`), keep only thin wrappers in handler/system, and have both call the shared orchestrator plus shared seam structs.

review::.::holistic::design_coherence::s.summary: Support request handling combines transport parsing, artifact collection, archiving, and reporting in one long function, making policy changes brittle.

evidence: `SupportHandlerWithRuntime` parses payload, validates/unmarshals JSON, creates filesystem roots, writes report data, invokes optional CPU profiling, creates zip, deletes temp directories, submits network payload, and emits transitions.
The same cohesive set of side effects exists as duplicated logic in the handler package variant, reinforcing that this concern has grown beyond one responsibility.
detail.suggestion: Split the workflow into explicit steps (`decodeInput`, `prepareWorkspace`, `collectArtifacts`, `packageArtifact`, `publishResult`) and inject side-effect adapters so each step is testable and policy can evolve independently.

review::.::holistic::design_coherence::p.summary: Runtime seam scaffolding is duplicated across subsystems, adding avoidable maintenance coupling and abstraction noise.

evidence: Both files define multiple operation structs with function fields and tiny wrappers (e.g., state/container/config/image/network/timer-style seams) that perform nil-check + forwarding behavior.
The pattern is repeated in production seams and mirrored via tests/stubs, suggesting a shared seam-construction convention is missing and likely to drift over time.
detail.suggestion: Introduce a shared seam helper layer or generic seam factory pattern (or small generated wrappers) that standardizes seam struct creation and default-noop behavior, then compose package-specific ops through composition not duplication.

responsibility_cohesion::goseg/shipworkf.summary: 11 disconnected function clusters (19 functions) — likely mixed responsibilities

subjective_review::goseg/orchestration/s.summary: No design review on record — run `desloppify review --prepare`

responsibility_cohesion::goseg/internal/.summary: 8 disconnected function clusters (46 functions) — likely mixed responsibilities

review::.::holistic::cross_module_archit.summary: Upload action contract is duplicated across protocol and upload service boundaries instead of being shared through one canonical type.

evidence: `goseg/protocol/actions/actions.go` defines `ActionUploadOpenEndpoint`, `ActionUploadReset`, namespace-aware parsing, and `UnsupportedActionError` for protocol actions.
The adapter test constructs payloads using `uploadsvc.ActionOpenEndpoint` / `uploadsvc.ActionReset` and validates `uploadsvc.UnsupportedActionError`, not the protocol action type path.
This introduces parallel action enums and duplicate transport-level validation semantics without explicit parity checks.
detail.suggestion: Create a single canonical action type used by both protocol parsing and uploadsvc command mapping (for example, re-export protocol actions from uploadsvc or move parse/validation into a shared boundary module), then delete duplicated action constants and keep explicit compile-time/per-module parity tests.

review::.::holistic::abstraction_fitness.summary: Default runtime seams hide behavior with silent no-op fallbacks, creating low-leverage indirection chains.

evidence: `newDockerRoutineRuntimeWithContext()` and `newDockerRuntimeWithContext()` populate many function fields through `seams.DefaultIfNil*`, and the seam methods (`GetState`, `StartContainer`, `UpdateContainerState`, etc.) are purely pass-through wrappers.
The fallback behavior in seam helpers allows missing collaborators to behave as no-op/zero-value instead of surfacing an immediate wiring error.
The same indirection pattern is repeated across multiple packages, adding several layers before reaching concrete operations.
detail.suggestion: Collapse seam abstractions to only the boundaries that need test seams: replace broad no-op-tolerant structs with explicit dependency interfaces for the few call sites requiring mockable edges, and make constructor requirements explicit (`panic`/error on missing dependency) so misconfiguration fails fast.

review::.::holistic::abstraction_fitness.summary: Startram default runtime injects placeholder callbacks that bypass real workflow steps in production handlers.

evidence: `HandleStartramRestart()` and `HandleStartramToggle()` call methods on `defaultStartramRuntime()` (`runtime.recoverWireguardFleetFn(...)`, `runtime.dispatchUrbitPayloadFn(...)`) so placeholder defaults are directly part of exported behavior.
`defaultStartramRuntime()` sets `recoverWireguardFleetFn` to a no-op `func(...) error { return nil }`, which prevents recovery actions from running in the normal execution path.
`defaultDispatchUrbitPayload` returns `fmt.Errorf("no urbit dispatcher configured")`, so toggle flows invoke an abstraction that is intentionally unimplemented unless swapped out before use.
detail.suggestion: Replace placeholder callback defaults with concrete, production-safe callbacks and isolate test-only overrides via explicit constructor injection (or option args), so default runtime has complete behavior and seam behavior cannot silently drop critical operations.

review::.::holistic::cross_module_archit.summary: Config runtime boundary seams are used inconsistently within the same module, weakening cross-module contract control.

evidence: `goseg/config/wireguard.go` defines `wireguardRuntime` and `newWireguardRuntime()`, but exported entry points are split: `GetWgConf` and `CreateDefaultWGConf` are directly coupled to concrete construction and filesystem actions, while `UpdateWGConf` and `CycleWgKey` route through injectable helpers.
This mismatch makes it possible for only part of the module to be substituted in tests or alternate hosts, which is an inconsistent cross-module boundary policy for the same config domain.
detail.suggestion: Unify `wireguard.go` into a single dependency-injected contract by exposing runtime-parameterized implementations for all exported entrypoints (`GetWgConf`, `CreateDefaultWGConf`, `UpdateWGConf`, `CycleWgKey`) and route each through one internal boundary function so callers share one stable module contract.

review::.::holistic::cross_module_archit.summary: Documented backend governance contract on explicit error propagation is not consistently applied in config runtime boundaries.

evidence: README backend expectations explicitly require explicit error propagation (`%w`) at boundaries.

`goseg/config/wireguard.go` returns wrapped errors in some places using `%w`, but `cycleWGKey` uses `%v` in at least two wrap sites, which drops causal chaining and weakens boundary-level diagnosability against the documented contract.
detail.suggestion: Normalize boundary error wrapping in `wireguard.go` to consistently use `%w` where upstream error context should remain inspectable, and add a short contract test to lock this policy on key boundary functions.

review::.::holistic::test_strategy::acti.summary: Upload protocol action contracts are under-tested at the parser boundary despite being listed in runtime contract surfaces.

evidence: The test file focuses on `CommandFromWsPayload` mapping but does not exercise `ParseUploadAction`, `ParseC2CAction`, or `Supported*Actions` in `goseg/protocol/actions/actions.go`.
The same batch README-oriented governance references these files in contract-gate coverage guidance, so parser-level contract regressions would be invisible here.
detail.suggestion: Add parser-focused tests covering supported/unsupported action enums, namespace isolation, and deterministic error type behavior, then add a narrow adapter test that asserts contract translation from parser outputs (not just raw payload string mapping).

review::.::holistic::test_strategy::adap.summary: Adapter test leaves the reset path only partially asserted, allowing contract drift in request shape and defaults.

evidence: `TestCommandFromWsPayloadMapsActionAndRequest` includes a `reset` case that asserts only `cmd.Action` equals expected and no request/error invariants.

No assertions verify reset command payload fields, token handling, or explicit failure modes when payload fields are missing/extra, so behavioral drift in reset contract can pass while still changing externally significant behavior.
detail.suggestion: Extend reset coverage with explicit expected request/state assertions (including token propagation and defaulted fields) and malformed reset payload cases, so the adapter remains coupled to contract semantics rather than only action enum equality.

review::.::holistic::design_coherence::d.summary: Importer returns success before post-import workflow outcome is representable, weakening API semantics.

evidence: `configureUploadedPier` invokes `runImportedPierWorkflow` directly and then starts `startImportedPierPostImportWorkflow` in a goroutine.
The goroutine only logs `log.Error` on failure and does not update a durable session or response contract that callers can observe.
There is no explicit completion signal to tie the API result to downstream workflow termination or failure.
detail.suggestion: Refactor import orchestration into a two-phase contract: persist session state transitions (requested -> workflow-running -> completed/failed), return a job id from the handler, and require the caller to observe completion through a status endpoint/event stream.

review::.::holistic::design_coherence::d.summary: Support handler couples control-flow and telemetry/event publishing via broad defer-based side effects.

evidence: The handler uses a deferred closure to publish error/success outcomes from shared local error state in addition to explicit publish calls.
Outcome publication is embedded in handler control-flow rather than a dedicated outcome translator, so event ordering and branch semantics become implicit.
This pattern makes handler behavior difficult to reason about when adding new branches.
detail.suggestion: Extract an explicit `SupportResult` state model and move publication into a dedicated post-handler coordinator that emits one terminal event per request path.

review::.::holistic::design_coherence::d.summary: Runtime seam packages duplicate the same adapter/constructor patterns instead of sharing a single seam abstraction.

evidence: Each listed seam file re-implements wrapper structs and defaulting helpers with very similar `DefaultIfNil`/constructor behavior.
Naming and wiring conventions are similar but not centralized, so behavior changes require synchronized edits across packages.
The repetition increases cognitive load and raises the risk of divergence in nil-safe boundaries.
detail.suggestion: Introduce a shared seam helper/factory module and a consistent dependency-injection contract so module-specific seams embed/compose common behavior instead of re-declaring equivalent defaults.

review::.::holistic::error_consistency::.summary: GetStoragePath suppresses directory creation failures and returns a usable-looking path with hidden failure state.

evidence: goseg/config/config.go:454-456: MkdirAll error is only fmt.Printf'ed and ignored; function still returns storagePath.
goseg/importer/importer.go:67-70 and many callers use GetStoragePath directly to allocate upload/temp directories.
detail.suggestion: Change GetStoragePath to return `(string, error)` or add a new checked variant and propagate failures through importer.Initialize and related startup call sites; only return fallback paths on explicitly whitelisted non-fatal conditions.

review::.::holistic::error_consistency::.summary: Broadcast startup drops StarTram region fetch errors and continues as if initialization succeeded.

evidence: goseg/broadcast/broadcast.go:83-86 logs LoadStartramRegions error and returns nil.
goseg/collectors/collectors.go:72-77 surfaces region-sync errors through function returns.
goseg/main.go:445-447 treats broadcast.Initialize() errors as fatal, but the region error path never bubbles up.
detail.suggestion: Decide and encode policy for region bootstrap: either return and propagate the region-load error from Initialize (with caller handling via warning-only mode), or add explicit degraded mode metadata so callers know broadcast region data is unavailable.

review::.::holistic::error_consistency::.summary: Logger initialization swallows directory-failure states in a no-return initialization function.

evidence: goseg/logger/logger.go:114-126 only prints when both mkdir attempts fail and exits initialization path without surfacing an error.
goseg/logger/logger.go:64 declares Initialize() with no return value, so startup cannot fail fast on logging setup failure.
goseg/main.go:467 calls logger.Initialize() without any post-check.
detail.suggestion: Make logger.Initialize return an error and have main/bootstrap decide whether to abort or fall back explicitly; if a fallback logger is intended, persist an explicit degraded state and emit a single summary error event to caller-visible lifecycle logs.

review::.::holistic::error_consistency::.summary: Accesspoint process status probe suppresses command failures and reports a false negative service state.

evidence: `isRunning()` executes `pgrep -af` and returns `false, nil` for any non-nil error, including command-not-found, permission errors, or execution failures.
`Start` and `Stop` use this boolean to drive control flow, so legitimate probe failures can trigger misleading start/stop decisions.
detail.suggestion: Differentiate expected `pgrep` 'no matches' behavior from runtime execution errors, return wrapped context for real failures, and keep probe failures explicit in the caller path.

review::.::holistic::error_consistency::.summary: Pre-start shell commands in accesspoint setup are executed but their errors are dropped, allowing partial initialization to continue.

evidence: `preStart()` assigns `res, err = executeRouterShellFn(...)` for each command and never checks `err` before continuing.
`startRouter()` calls `preStart()` and proceeds to configure interfaces/firewall/services regardless of whether preparatory commands failed.
`executeShell` wraps failures as `command failed: <stderr>` and discards wrapped command context.
detail.suggestion: Propagate each preparatory command error with context, gate startup on successful preconditions, and preserve command, exit status, and stderr in wrapped errors for diagnostics.

review::.::holistic::error_consistency::.summary: Auth lockout counters are exposed through lockless reads, creating data races and stale lockout telemetry.

evidence: `LoginHandler` mutates `failedLogins`/`remainder` under `loginMu` but `UnauthHandler()` reads `remainder` directly without synchronization.
`enforceLockout()` mutates `remainder` under lock while other callers can read it concurrently.
detail.suggestion: Read and write lockout state through a single synchronized accessor layer (mutex/atomic), and emit a dedicated lockout state snapshot object rather than raw package globals.

review::.::holistic::cross_module_archit.summary: Multiple packages introduce compatibility-style shims that re-export internals instead of defining meaningful module boundaries.

evidence: `auth/auth.go` only exports aliases and wrappers to `authsvc` symbols.
`docker/docker.go` forwards almost every function to `docker/orchestration` with no policy, translation, or lifecycle wrapping.
`docker/events.go` forwards every event publish/consume function to `docker/events`.
detail.suggestion: Replace trivial pass-through packages with direct imports at call sites, and keep a narrow, explicit adapter layer only where API shielding or contract translation is required.

review::.::holistic::abstraction_fitness.summary: Several cross-cutting abstractions are too thin to add leverage, increasing churn while reducing clarity.

evidence: Command orchestration in accesspoint is still built from raw shell strings at call sites without typed command abstraction.
`handler/api/entrypoint.go` and `auth/auth.go` simply alias existing entry points instead of exposing a distinct service contract.
The indirection does not reduce coupling to lower-level modules, it only shifts call paths.
detail.suggestion: Replace stringly-typed shell orchestration with typed command runners and collapse unnecessary alias layers; keep interfaces only where behavior differs per environment.

review::.::holistic::cross_module_archit.summary: uploadsvc leaks protocol action/error types instead of owning its own command contract boundary.

evidence: service.go defines `type Action = actions.Action` and `type UnsupportedActionError = actions.UnsupportedActionError`, exposing protocol package types through uploadsvc.
the upload service parser and dispatcher are thin pass-throughs to protocol-level types, and adapter tests refer to protocol action identifiers directly.
detail.suggestion: Define uploadsvc-local action/error contracts and convert to/from protocol actions at the adapter boundary (single façade). Keep `uploadsvc` exports independent of `protocol/actions` by introducing explicit adapter translation functions and return uploadsvc-owned error types.

review::.::holistic::cross_module_archit.note: not reported in latest holistic re-import

review::.::holistic::abstraction_fitness.summary: There is a pervasive pass-through seam layer duplicated across modules that adds little behavior and dilutes abstraction leverage.

evidence: The routines layer declares global dependency vars such as `getContainerStateForRoutine`, `startContainerForRoutine`, `getShipStatusForRoutine`, then exposes method wrappers that only call `seams.RequireSeam*` (`goseg/routines/runtime_seams.go`).

Docker orchestration defines an equivalent set (`openForRuntime`, `startContainerForRuntime`, `getContainerRunningStatusForRuntime`, etc.) with equivalent wrapper structs and `RequireSeam` forwarding (`goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_seams.go`).

`broadcast/broadcast.go` and `shipworkflow/startram.go` repeat the same pattern for runtime/DI via struct fields whose defaults are wrappers over direct package functions without policy/value-added behavior.
The result is duplicate interface surfaces where the abstraction boundary appears to exist but does not enforce stronger contracts or behavior.
detail.suggestion: Consolidate these seam layers into targeted interfaces per cohesive responsibility (e.g., lifecycle, orchestration, broadcast, config), and provide small, explicit adapters only where behavior translation is needed; remove thin function-by-function wrappers that provide zero policy/value addition.

review::.::holistic::abstraction_fitness.note: not reported in latest holistic re-import

review::.::holistic::abstraction_fitness.summary: Testability seams are coupled to package-global mutable state, which increases test coupling and increases maintenance cost versus stable dependency injection.

evidence: `routines/docker_test.go` constructs type aliases of runtime structs and manually rebuilds every function field (`testDockerRoutineRuntime`) to inject behavior.
`routines/system_test.go` snapshots and restores package-level seam variables via `resetSystemSeamsForTest`, showing per-test mutation of global dependency holders.
`shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go` also exposes package-level function vars with mutator (`SetAliasCNAMELookup`, `SetUrbitCleanupSeams`) rather than immutable constructor-based dependencies.
This pattern requires each module to remember separate mutation/reset conventions in tests instead of relying on one stable seam contract.
detail.suggestion: Replace mutable package-level seam vars with explicit runtime factory functions that accept dependency providers as constructor arguments, and keep tests passing stable fake implementations through those constructors; keep package globals readonly in production.

review::.::holistic::abstraction_fitness.note: not reported in latest holistic re-import

review::.::holistic::abstraction_fitness.summary: A runtime function field advertises error signaling but discards execution errors, weakening interface honesty at the abstraction boundary.

evidence: `broadcastRuntime.broadcastAuth` in `broadcast/broadcast.go` has signature `func(payload []byte) error` but always returns `nil` after calling `auth.GetClientManager().BroadcastAuth(payload)`, regardless of failures.
This wrapper is part of the runtime abstraction path used by callers expecting an error-aware contract, but the contract is not actually honored.
Any seam/wrapper in the stack that promises error propagation but doesn't enforce it blurs behavior and encourages silent failure handling in callers.
detail.suggestion: Propagate and wrap errors from underlying auth broadcast operations (or remove `error` from the interface if broadcast is intentionally fire-and-forget) so the abstraction contract matches actual behavior.

review::.::holistic::abstraction_fitness.note: not reported in latest holistic re-import

`uploadsvc/service.go` separately defines `actionBindings`/`SupportedActions` behavior for the same action namespace.
`uploadsvc/service.go` re-exports `Action` and `UnsupportedActionError` instead of isolating a local, explicit command contract.
detail.suggestion: Introduce a single source of truth for upload actions (e.g., centralized action metadata in `protocol/actions`) and derive both parser validation and executor bindings from it, removing duplicated/manual maintenance and making contract drift impossible.

review::.::holistic::test_strategy::rese.summary: Adapter tests codify permissive reset payload passthrough while dispatch ignores it, missing action-shape invariants.

evidence: Case `reset-with-extra-fields` expects `Endpoint`, `Remote`, `Fix`, `SelectedDrive` to be copied into `OpenEndpointRequest`.
`actionBindings` reset handler ignores `OpenEndpointRequest` fields and calls only `service.Reset()`, so reset-time payload shape is effectively unconstrained.
No tests in this batch assert that reset input should be rejected, sanitized, or explicitly separated from open-endpoint request fields.
detail.suggestion: Replace the reset test scenario with action-specific contract tests: define strict per-action payload parsing rules (e.g., allowed field set and defaults), test that reset does not accept unrelated transport fields, and add a protocol-level test for allowed/disallowed action payloads.

review::.::holistic::design_coherence::w.summary: Duplicate wireguard fleet recovery logic is duplicated across handler and workflow modules

evidence: `recoverWireguardFleet` in `goseg/shipworkflow/startram.go` and `RecoverWireguardFleet` in `goseg/handler/system/recovery.go` perform the same restart sequence: collect ship status, restart `wireguard`, optionally issue `click.BarExit`/`WaitForShipExit`, delete ship/minio containers, then reload urbits/mc/minios.
Message text differs slightly (for example, `restart Wireguard` vs `restart wireguard` and minor condition formatting), indicating copy drift risk without single shared ownership.
detail.suggestion: Extract one `WireguardRecoveryOrchestrator` in a single package (`orchestration` or new `recovery` package) with a small options struct, and have both call sites delegate to it so sequencing and error aggregation are centralized.

`goseg/handler/system/recovery.go` already contains long orchestration-style control flow for fleet recovery, suggesting mixed handler/orchestration boundaries are recurring in the same module family.
detail.suggestion: Split support reporting into explicit steps with boundaries: `decodeSupportRequest`, `collectArtifacts`, `archiveReport`, and `submitReport`, then compose them in a small coordinator; this makes each step independently testable and easier to evolve.

review::.::holistic::error_consistency::.summary: `UnmarshalJSON` ignores JSON field type mismatches and populates defaults, making config corruption hard to detect

evidence: `goseg/structs/configs.go` uses many assignments like `u.UrbitVersion, _ = v.(string)` and `u.MeldSchedule, _ = v.(bool)`, discarding assertion failures.
`toInt`/`toBool`/`toString` helpers return zero defaults on invalid input instead of surfacing an error.
Downstream initialization paths (`loadSystemConfigOrDefault` in `goseg/config/config.go`) deserialize config from disk and continue without surfacing these parse violations.
detail.suggestion: Refactor `UrbitDocker.UnmarshalJSON` to validate each field with explicit `ok` checks and return an error containing the offending key/type; avoid zero-default fallbacks and fail the config load/update when malformed config is encountered.

review::.::holistic::error_consistency::.note: not reported in latest holistic re-import

review::.::holistic::error_consistency::.summary: Entropy failures are converted to empty secrets, weakening auth and config security without error propagation

evidence: `goseg/config/config.go` implements `RandString` to return `""` when `rand.Read` fails.
Callers such as `config.seedDefaultConfigRuntimeState`, `config.ensureSessionKeyExists`, and `auth.CreateToken` in `goseg/auth/auth.go` consume `RandString` results without checking for failure.
No caller-side guard prevents zero-length key/secret/id values from being persisted or issued.
detail.suggestion: Change `RandString` to return `(string, error)` and propagate failures through config/auth call sites so startup or token/session creation fails fast when entropy generation fails.

review::.::holistic::error_consistency::.note: not reported in latest holistic re-import

review::.::holistic::cross_module_archit.summary: Unsafe shell string assembly bypasses typed command execution in accesspoint

evidence: executeRouterCommand builds a string via formatShellCommand(name, args...) and calls executeRouterShellCommand -> executeShell which uses `exec.Command("sh", "-c", commandString)`.
Both preStart/startRouter/stopRouter rely on this string form, including optional args (e.g., interface names/IP strings) interpolated into command text.
detail.suggestion: Replace string assembly with argument-safe execution: change `executeShell` API to accept command name and arg list, drop `sh -c`, and remove `formatShellCommand`. Keep a small explicit compatibility shim only where shell syntax is truly required.

review::.::holistic::cross_module_archit.note: not reported in latest holistic re-import

review::.::holistic::error_consistency::.summary: Seam default fallback can panic and bring down runtime instead of returning an actionable error

evidence: DefaultIfNil*/RequireSeam* helper paths return closures that call `panicOnNilSeamFn` when required seam function is missing.
Runtime seam methods in `runtime_seams.go` invoke these closures directly, so misconfigured seams become panic paths in production execution.
detail.suggestion: Shift missing-seam behavior to explicit startup validation and error-returning boundaries: initialize all required seams eagerly in a constructor, fail fast with config diagnostics, and make runtime methods return clear errors instead of panicking.

review::.::holistic::error_consistency::.summary: Hoon-file cleanup failures are ignored, masking operational issues

evidence: `deleteHoon` in both files checks existence with `os.Stat` and calls `os.Remove` without checking returned error.
Callers use these delete helpers from deferred cleanup paths, so silent failures can accumulate stale files and hide permission/disk failures.
detail.suggestion: Make delete helpers return error; propagate and aggregate cleanup errors where command/execution failures are handled. At minimum log deletion failures with full path context and ship identifier.

review::.::holistic::abstraction_fitness.summary: Runtime seam layers are duplicated across packages with mostly pass-through behavior, increasing indirection and cognitive load.

evidence: `routines/runtime_seams.go` defines `dockerRoutineRuntime` and `dockerRoutineStateOps`/`dockerRoutineContainerOps` wrappers where most fields are direct passthroughs to module-level functions.
`docker/orchestration/runtime_seams.go` defines a parallel seam graph (`dockerRuntime`, `dockerRuntimeContextOps`, `dockerRuntimeContainerOps`, etc.) with equivalent pass-through initialization.
Both files create largely parallel abstractions for similar boundaries without evidence of policy separation beyond seam injection/testing indirection.
detail.suggestion: Introduce a shared seam-building utility or unified runtime abstraction layer so orchestration and routine call-sites share a small set of explicit constructor arguments instead of duplicated per-package op-struct scaffolding.
detail.investigation:
Investigating `runtime_seam_boilerplate` (ID `review::.::holistic::abstraction_fitness::runtime_seam_boilerplate::d07ae05c`).

`goseg/routines/runtime_seams.go` and `goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_seams.go` each define largely similar seam fields for common runtime dependencies (file/path/container/context/exec operations), often as thin wrappers.

Planned direction:
- Replace duplicated seam scaffolding with shared seam builders in `goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_seams.go` (new common seam type(s) or helper builders).
- Ensure `routines` and `orchestration` consume these shared seams instead of maintaining separate parallel op-struct layers.
- Preserve testability: keep field overrides for tests, but remove duplicated constructor boilerplate.
